metadata.course_name: Stanford CS336 Language Modeling from Scratch

discussions.title: Lecture introduction, goals and logistics
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section introduces the lecture, its goals, and class logistics. The instructor explains that this lecture will cover detailed transformer architecture and training choices, and that students should update assignment code from the website.

{Definitions}:
- Lecture goals: The plan to explain detailed design choices in large language model (LLM) architectures and training, so students learn from many published models' experience.

{Key Concepts}:
- The lecture focuses on practical, nitty-gritty architecture and hyperparameter choices used in real LLMs.
- The instructor will review a transformer, show a modern variant, then analyze many recent model choices to find common useful practices.

{Important Notes}:
- You should pull updates to assignments from the course website (there are minor bug fixes).
- The lecture will be more detailed than some courses — expect PowerPoint PDFs rather than runnable Python notebooks.

discussions.title: Transformer review and the implemented variant
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This part reviews the original transformer structure and explains the course implementation variant. The instructor lists the standard blocks (position embeddings, multi-head attention, layer norms, residual stream, MLP, softmax) and notes key changes students implement: pre-layer-normalization, RoPE position embeddings, gated feed-forward (SwiGlu/Swigloo), and removal of many bias terms.

{Definitions}:
- Transformer: A neural network architecture for sequence modeling that stacks attention layers, feed-forward layers, layer normalization, and residual (skip) connections.
- Position embeddings: Vectors added or applied to token embeddings so the model knows token order.
- Multi-head attention: An attention mechanism that splits model dimensions into several 'heads' to attend in parallel to different aspects of the input.
- Residual stream (or residual connection): A shortcut that adds layer input to its output to help gradients flow through deep networks.
- MLP (feed-forward layer): The small fully-connected network inside each transformer block that processes each token independently.
- Softmax: A function that turns raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution.
- Pre-norm (pre-layer-normalization): Putting layer normalization before each sub-block inside the residual path.
- RoPE (rotary position embeddings): A method of adding relative position information by rotating query/key vectors based on position.
- SwiGlu / Swigloo: A gated feed-forward variant that uses a specific activation (SwiGLU-like) and gating to improve performance.

{Key Concepts}:
- Modern transformer variants tweak many internal choices (where norms go, activation functions, positional encodings, bias usage).
- The course implementation follows a widely used

discussions.title: Survey of recent model releases and convergent trends
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
The instructor surveys many recent LLM papers and shows patterns that appear repeatedly across model releases. This survey highlights that some design choices converge (become common) while many others vary, and that studying many models helps find what matters.

{Definitions}:
- Convergent evolution (in architectures): Different teams independently making the same design choices because those choices work well in practice.

{Key Concepts}:
- Over the years, many models tested different options (absolute vs relative positional encodings, different norms, activation choices), but many now converge on similar defaults.
- Collecting this evidence helps pick sensible defaults rather than guessing hyperparameters.

{Important Notes}:
- Expect to update the lecture every year: the model landscape changes fast.
- The instructor compiled a spreadsheet tracking architecture changes from 2017 to 2025 to spot trends.

discussions.title: Lecture roadmap and architecture topics to cover
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This short segment previews three main lecture parts: architecture variations, hyperparameter choices, and stability/attention variants. The instructor outlines the subtopics to be discussed, like activations, feed-forwards, attention variants, and positional embeddings.

{Definitions}:
- Architecture variations: Different ways to build transformer blocks (norms, activations, MLP types, attention patterns).
- Hyperparameters: Tunable choices like hidden size, feed-forward size, number of heads, and vocabulary size.

{Key Concepts}:
- The lecture will first survey architecture variants, then discuss how to choose hyperparameters, then cover training stability and attention variants.

{Important Notes}:
- Some topics (attention variants) may come at the end if there is time; the lecture is content-heavy.

discussions.title: Pre-norm vs post-norm, training stability, and the "double norm" idea
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section explains where to put layer normalization and why many models moved from post-norm to pre-norm. Pre-norm is easier to train without special warm-up and reduces loss/gradient spikes. A newer variant adds layer norms both before and after blocks ("double norm") to further improve stability in large models.

{Definitions}:
- Post-norm: Placing layer normalization after each sub-block when adding it back to the residual stream (older transformer choice).
- Pre-norm: Placing layer normalization before each sub-block, outside the residual connection (modern standard because it is more stable).
- Double norm (informal name used by instructor): Putting layer normalization both before and after non-residual sub-blocks to improve training stability in some large models.
- Learning rate warm-up: Slowly increasing the learning rate at the start of training to avoid instability.

{Key Concepts}:
- Post-norm often required careful learning-rate warm-up and could show unstable training with large gradient spikes.
- Pre-norm generally yields more stable gradients and removes the need for complicated warm-up schedules in many cases.
- The double-norm variant (layers both before and after blocks) has appeared in recent models and can further reduce instability for very large models.

{Important Notes}:
- The main practical lesson: use pre-norm (layer norms outside the residual) for better training stability.
- The double-norm variation is emerging; it may make large-model training easier but is still under active exploration.
- An exception (e.g., OPT 350M) exists where post-norm was used and caused problems during training; exceptions can happen.

discussions.title: LayerNorm formula, RMSNorm, and dropping bias terms
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This part explains LayerNorm math, why many models switched to RMSNorm, and why modern models often remove bias terms. RMSNorm is simpler and slightly faster because it skips mean subtraction and beta (bias) shifts, and removing biases can help optimization stability and reduce memory movement.

{Definitions}:
- LayerNorm: A normalization that subtracts the mean of activations and divides by their standard deviation, then scales (gamma) and shifts (beta) the result.
- RMSNorm: A simpler normalization that divides by the root mean square (standard deviation) but does not subtract the mean or add a shift; it uses fewer operations and parameters.
- Bias term (in linear layers): A learned vector added after a linear layer; many modern models drop these to simplify computation and sometimes improve stability.

{Key Concepts}:
- RMSNorm often matches LayerNorm in final performance but costs fewer memory operations, which can speed up runtime because memory movement matters a lot on GPUs.
- Even small reductions in memory movement (fewer parameters to load/store) can reduce runtime significantly, because non-matrix-multiply ops can dominate runtime despite few FLOPs.
- Dropping bias terms simplifies the model (fewer parameters and memory accesses) and empirically sometimes stabilizes optimization for large-scale training.

{Important Notes}:
- Use RMSNorm when possible: it's widely adopted (LLaMA family, PaLM, Chinchilla, T5) and is a practical default.
- Avoid assuming FLOPs are the only cost: memory movement and loading parameters matter a lot for runtime.
- Not every model follows these choices; a few (e.g., some proprietary models or older ones) may still use full LayerNorm or keep biases.

discussions.title: Activations and gated linear units (GLUs) such as SwiGlu/Swigloo
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section reviews activation functions and why gated linear units (GLUs) like SwiGLU are now common. GLUs multiply an activation by a learned gate term entry-wise. Empirical studies show GLU variants typically give lower loss or better downstream performance, so many modern LLMs use them.

{Definitions}:
- Activation function (ReLU, GELU, Swish, etc.): A nonlinear function applied element-wise inside neural network layers.
- ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): max(0, x), a simple activation that zeros out negatives.
- GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit): An activation similar to ReLU but smoother; it multiplies x by the Gaussian CDF of x.
- Swish (SiLU): x * sigmoid(x), another smooth activation.
- Gated Linear Unit (GLU): A feed-forward design that gates one transform by another; often splits and multiplies element-wise so the network can control flow per component.
- SwiGlu / Swigloo: A specific GLU variant combining Swish-like activation and gating; widely used in modern models.

{Key Concepts}:
- GLUs add a learned gate (an extra projection) that multiplies the main activation output, giving more expressive control inside the MLP.
- To keep parameter counts similar, gated models usually reduce hidden sizes (e.g., scale D_ff down by ~2/3) so the total parameters match non-gated counterparts.
- Many ablation studies (Nang et al., Nom Shazir) show GLU variants consistently improve performance over plain ReLU or GELU in multiple tasks.

{Important Notes}:
- GLUs are not strictly necessary—some very strong models (e.g., GPT-3, Neotron, Falcon) used other activations— but GLUs are a practical, common choice in post-2023 models.
- Gated activations add a small amount of extra compute but memory movement is usually the dominant cost, so runtime impact is small.
- When switching to GLU, adjust D_ff (feed-forward hidden size) to keep parameter count comparable (e.g., use 8/3 * D_model for GLU when starting from a 4x rule).

discussions.title: Serial vs parallel transformer blocks
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section contrasts serial transformer blocks (attention then MLP) with parallel block designs (compute attention and MLP in parallel and add results). Parallel blocks can improve systems efficiency by fusing computations but may be less common; most recent models still use serial blocks.

{Definitions}:
- Serial transformer block: A block that runs attention first, then passes the output into the MLP (sequential composition of sub-blocks).
- Parallel transformer block: A block that computes attention and the MLP from the same input in parallel, then sums their outputs into the residual stream.

{Key Concepts}:
- Parallel layers allow fusing some operations (layer norms, matrix multiplies) and can improve GPU utilization and system efficiency by enabling more parallel compute.
- Serial composition might be slightly more expressive (composition of transforms), but parallel forms trade some expressiveness for runtime gains.

{Important Notes}:
- Parallel blocks were popularized in some open-source and large-scale projects (e.g., GPT-J, PaLM) but many models still use serial blocks.
- When choosing parallel vs serial, consider system-level trade-offs like GPU utilization and kernel fusion opportunities.

discussions.title: Position embeddings and RoPE (Rotary Positional Embeddings)
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This segment explains different position embedding choices and why many models prefer RoPE (rotary positional embeddings). RoPE applies rotations to query and key vectors so inner products depend only on relative position differences. RoPE is applied on queries and keys (inside attention), not at the bottom token embeddings.

{Definitions}:
- Absolute position embeddings: Learned vectors added to token embeddings to encode token position directly.
- Relative position embeddings: Methods that encode distance between tokens rather than absolute positions.
- RoPE (Rotary Positional Embeddings): A method that rotates pairs of dimensions of query/key vectors by an angle depending on token position so inner products reflect relative positions only.
- Inner product invariance to rotation: The property that the dot product between two vectors doesn't change if both are rotated by the same transformation; RoPE uses this to encode relative offsets.

{Key Concepts}:
- RoPE enforces attention behavior that depends on relative distances by rotating query/key vectors before computing attention scores.
- RoPE is implemented by splitting high-dimensional vectors into 2D pairs and rotating each pair by position-dependent angles (fast/slow frequencies across dimensions) so some pairs capture short-range and others long-range patterns.
- RoPE is applied when generating queries and keys (inside attention) rather than adding vectors at the input embedding layer.

{Important Notes}:
- RoPE has become a widely adopted default for position encoding in many modern models because it captures relative positions and supports context-length extrapolation.
- The rotation rates (thetas) are not trained; they follow a fixed schedule like sinusoidal encodings.

discussions.title: Hyperparameters: feed-forward size (D_ff), D_model ratios, and evidence
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section explains common choices for feed-forward size relative to model dimension and empirical evidence for those ratios. The usual choice is D_ff = 4 * D_model for non-gated MLPs; gated models scale D_ff down (commonly to about 8/3 * D_model) to keep parameter counts similar. Scaling-law experiments show this area has a broad basin where many ratios work well.

{Definitions}:
- D_model: The hidden dimension size of the transformer (size of token vectors in the residual stream).
- D_ff (feed-forward dimension): The inner dimension (hidden size) inside the MLP of a transformer block; usually an up-projection before down-projection.
- GLU parameter-matched scaling (rough rule): For gated MLPs, reduce D_ff so total parameters stay similar; commonly D_ff ≈ 8/3 * D_model when starting from D_ff = 4 * D_model.

{Key Concepts}:
- The canonical rule is D_ff = 4 * D_model for simple MLPs; gated MLPs reduce D_ff to keep param counts similar (around 2.6–2.7× D_model in many models).
- Some models intentionally choose much larger D_ff (e.g., T5 used a 64× multiplier) to favor very wide MLPs, which can be beneficial but are not required.
- Kaplan et al.'s scaling experiments show a wide basin of near-optimal ratios (roughly 1–10×) so 4× is a practical default.

{Important Notes}:
- If you use GLU, reduce D_ff to match parameters (the course assignments ask students to follow the gated variant used by many modern models).
- T5 and some other models are deliberate outliers that chose very large D_ff; such choices can work but may be less standard.

discussions.title: Head dimension ratios, model aspect ratio (depth vs width), and evidence
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This discussion covers how to set head dimensions and the model's depth vs width (aspect ratio). The usual rule is to keep D_model = num_heads * head_dim (a 1:1 ratio so head_dim = D_model / num_heads). Empirical studies show an aspect-ratio sweet spot around ~100 hidden units per layer for many scales.

{Definitions}:
- num_heads: The number of separate attention heads inside multi-head attention.
- head_dim: The size of each attention head; typically head_dim = D_model / num_heads under the common convention.
- Aspect ratio (in transformer context): The ratio between model width (D_model) and number of layers (depth); it reflects whether a model is wider or deeper for a fixed parameter budget.

{Key Concepts}:
- Most models set head_dim so that the total D_model splits evenly across heads; this keeps parameter allocation balanced across heads.
- Some research warns that very small head_dim per head could reduce expressiveness (low-rank attention), but practical models with the 1:1 convention perform well.
- Kaplan-style experiments and other studies find an optimal region where having about ~100 hidden dimensions per layer (D_model / depth) works well across scales.

{Important Notes}:
- T5 was an exception in several design choices (including head ratios); exceptions exist but most recent successful models follow these defaults.
- Systems and parallelism constraints (pipeline vs tensor parallelism) can affect choices: width and depth interact with how you distribute work across GPUs.

discussions.title: Vocabulary size trends and regularization choices (dropout vs weight decay)
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This part explains that vocabulary sizes have trended upward (often 100k–250k) especially for production/multilingual models, and discusses why dropout has fallen out of fashion while weight decay remains common. Large vocabularies help cover many languages and tokens (emojis, etc.), and weight decay helps training dynamics (not primarily to prevent overfitting) during long pretraining runs.

{Definitions}:
- Vocabulary size (token count): Number of distinct tokens the tokenizer maps text into; larger vocabularies split languages and symbols more finely.
- Dropout: A regularization technique that randomly zeroes out activations during training to reduce overfitting.
- Weight decay: L2 regularization on model weights that reduces parameter magnitudes during training, sometimes used to affect optimization dynamics.

{Key Concepts}:
- Production and multilingual models use larger vocabularies to better represent many languages and symbols; for high-resource single-language models smaller vocabularies can be enough.
- Dropout is less common in modern pretraining because pretraining involves very large datasets and usually one pass over data, so overfitting isn’t the main issue.
- Weight decay is still used because it interacts with learning-rate schedules and helps improve final training optimization dynamics, especially near the tail of training.

{Important Notes}:
- Weight decay's benefit for LLM pretraining is not mainly reducing overfitting; instead it changes optimizer dynamics and can give better training loss during learning-rate decay.
- There is not a strong published consensus explaining why dropout faded; empirically it is less used in large-scale pretraining.

discussions.title: Training stability problems and fixes: softmax issues, Z-loss, QK-norm, and soft capping
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section shows how instability (large gradient spikes) can stop training and discusses practical fixes. Softmax-related operations (output softmax and attention softmax) can cause numerical and optimization issues. Common fixes include adding a Z-loss (regularizer on softmax normalizer), applying normalization to queries/keys (QK-norm), and soft-capping logits.

{Definitions}:
- Softmax (in attention and output): The exponential-and-normalize function used for attention weights and output token probabilities; it can be numerically unstable when inputs are large.
- Z-loss (auxiliary loss): A penalty term added to encourage the softmax normalizer Z to be near 1 (or log Z near 0), which reduces numerical instability at the output softmax.
- QK-norm: Applying layer normalization to queries and keys before computing attention scores, which bounds inputs to softmax and reduces spikes.
- Soft capping of logits: Passing logits through a function (e.g., scaled tanh) to limit extreme values before softmax.

{Key Concepts}:
- Large gradient spikes often come from softmax operations (exponentials and divisions) and can cause training to fail.
- Z-loss directly regularizes the output softmax normalizer to be stable, converting a softmax into a more numerically stable form when successful.
- QK-norm stabilizes attention softmax by normalizing queries and keys before computing dot products, reducing large score magnitudes.
- Soft capping can clip extreme logits but may hurt performance in some setups; QK-norm is often more effective.

{Important Notes}:
- Many recent models (PaLM, Gemma 2, DCLM, others) adopted Z-loss and QK-norm as practical stability fixes.
- These fixes are especially important when training large or multimodal models or when adding new modalities because those cause sudden distribution shifts.

discussions.title: Inference optimizations: KV cache, arithmetic intensity, MQA/GQA, and long-context attention patterns
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This closing section explains inference costs and optimizations: the KV cache, arithmetic intensity trade-offs, multi-query attention (MQA) and group-query attention (GQA), and techniques for long context like sparse or sliding-window attention. MQA/GQA reduce memory movement for keys/values during autoregressive generation and structured attention patterns help extend context length while keeping compute feasible.

{Definitions}:
- KV cache: Storing past keys and values during autoregressive generation so the model only computes new queries each step instead of recomputing everything.
- Arithmetic intensity: The ratio of arithmetic operations (FLOPs) to memory accesses; higher is better for GPU efficiency.
- Multi-Query Attention (MQA): An attention design where multiple query heads are used but keys and values are shared (single K/V), reducing memory movement for KV cache.
- Group-Query Attention (GQA): A compromise where keys and values are reduced to a smaller number of groups (not fully shared), balancing memory and expressiveness.
- Sparse / sliding-window attention: Attention patterns that only look at nearby tokens or follow a fixed sparse set of connections to reduce the quadratic cost in sequence length.

{Key Concepts}:
- Autoregressive inference uses the KV cache and computes attention one new row at a time, which changes memory-access patterns and lowers arithmetic intensity compared to training.
- The KV cache causes repeated memory movement of keys/values; MQA reduces those moves by sharing K/V across query heads, improving throughput at inference.
- GQA gives a middle ground between full multi-head attention and MQA, letting designers trade expressiveness for efficiency.
- For very long context, modern models combine techniques: occasional full self-attention layers (no position encodings) plus several local-window layers with RoPE; this mix helps extrapolate to long contexts efficiently.

{Important Notes}:
- MQA and GQA change model behavior slightly; some papers report little loss in quality for large gains in inference speed and memory use.
- Long-context designs (sparse, sliding-window, hybrid blocks) are active design areas; different teams use different patterns (e.g., GPT-3 used sparse patterns; recent LLaMA variants use hybrid block patterns).
- When optimizing inference, arithmetic intensity and memory movement are the central constraints — choose attention patterns and head designs with GPU memory and networking in mind.
